items.title: Tonga: Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report #19, 12th May 2026 [EN/TO]
items.description:
Country: Tonga
Sources: Government of Tonga, Tonga Red Cross Society

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS:

• A total of 60 confirmed cases since the last report; including 2 new case.

• Age group, gender, and island distribution remain unchanged, with the majority of cases reported among age group 11 -14years, predominantly male, and highest in Tongatapu Island.

• No cases required hospitalization during this reporting period.

• No Dengue- related death reported to date.

• Dengue virus serotype 2 (DENV-2)

items.title: DR Congo: République démocratique du Congo : Cash Working Group Réunion de avril 2026 [Meeting minutes]
items.description:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached files.

Entre janvier et mars 2025, selon les informations rapportées au CWG, 120 000 personnes ont été atteintes par une réponse cash en RDC pour un montant de 4.6 millions de dollars desquels 2 millions ont été pour du cash sectoriel et 2.6 millions pour des transferts d’espèces à usages multiples (TEUM).
Concernant la cible TEUM, 57 000 personnes du total de 2.2 millions ciblés en 2026 ont été atteintes par les TEUM, représentant un 3% de couverture.

items.title: DR Congo: République démocratique du Congo : Cash Working Group Réunion de mars 2026 [Meeting minutes]
items.description:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

Présentation des résultats de l’analyse de marchés (ICSM – février 2026)

• Le coût médian du MEB, calculé sur l’ensemble des marchés évalués, est resté stable entre janvier et février et il s’élevait à 381'196 francs congolais.
• Dans le territoire de Mahagi, la majorité des marchés suivis (4 sur 5) ont enregistré une forte hausse du coût médian du MEB entre janvier et février.
• En février, les marchés évalués dans le territoire d'Uvira au Sud-Kivu ont enregistré des hausses du coût médian du MEB.
• Les marchés évalués dans le territoire de Kalehe avaient globalement des problèmes graves de fonctionnalité.
• Grâce au soutien des partenaires ICSM, la couverture a été étendue à 18 nouveaux marchés dans l’Ouest de la RDC, en particulier dans les provinces du Kwango, Kwilu et Maï-Ndombe.
Les résultats du suivi des marchés de REACH (ICSM) de janvier peuvent être consultés ici :
-Base de données : https://repository.impact-initiatives.org/document/impact/3e434a51/REACH_RDC_Base-de-donnees_ICSM_Fevrier_2026-1.xlsx
- Fiche d’information : https://repository.impact-initiatives.org/document/impact/4f7f5419/REACH-RDC-ICSM-Factsheet-Fevrier-2026_v2.pdf
REACH a aussi publié une dashboard où toutes les informations peuvent être consultés y compris les données historiques.

items.title: DR Congo: RDC — Rapport d'enregistrement et enquêtes d'intentions : 39 sites à Rutshuru et Masisi, Nord-Kivu (avril 2026)
items.description:
Country: Democratic Republic of the Congo
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

Contexte

La province du Nord-Kivu, en particulier le territoire de Rutshuru, demeure affectée par une insécurité persistante, principalement liée aux attaques de groupes armés et aux conflits intercommunautaires, entraînant des déplacements prolongés de population. Dans ce contexte, la Matrice de Suivi des Déplacements (DTM) de l’Organisation Internationale pour les Migrations (OIM) a mené des opérations d’enregistrement et de vérification, ainsi qu’une enquête sur les intentions, dans 39 sites spontanés des territoires du Rutshuru et Masisi. Ces évaluations ont été réalisées à travers un exercice de collecte de données conduit du 19 au 25 avril 2026. Cette opération a permis d’enregistrer 3 231 ménages, soit 10 213 individus, représentant une partie limitée des personnes déplacées internes présentes dans la province.

items.title: Kazakhstan — Migration Situation Report 13 (October - December 2025)
items.description:
Country: Kazakhstan
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

I. ABOUT MOBILITY TRACKING MATRIX

Mobility Tracking Matrix (MTM) is a system based on IOM's Global Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM),1 and aims to track and monitor population mobility. MTM collects and analyzes information about mobility and vulnerabilities of mobile populations. This system facilitates the systematic collection and dissemination of information to key decision-makers on the needs of these populations. The quarterly report is developed by combining secondary data obtained from different sources, including government agencies, international organizations, non-profit entities and other types of organizations. More specifically, the report for the fourth quarter of 2025 was derived from data from the Ministry of Internal Affairs, Bureau of National Statistics, and Border Service of the National Security Committee of the Republic of Kazakhstan. The data was compiled through a combination of published sources and direct requests to the partners and government agencies. 1. Displacement Tracking Matrix website: https://dtm.iom.int/.

I.I METHODOLOGY The report focuses on the most relevant topics related to migration trends in Kazakhstan using currently available and reliable datasets. It does not aim to address all migration topics. Limitations of the data may arise due to (1) the unavailability of some data for the indicated period, (2) the incompatible disaggregation or breakdown with other data, (3) the methodology not being publicly available or (4) the data collection/reporting periods not always aligning. For clarification, please refer to the sources listed in the bibliography.

items.title: Niger — Enquête sur les intentions futures des personnes déplacées internes (Février 2026)
items.description:
Country: Niger
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

RÉSUMÉ

La gestion des personnes déplacées internes constitue une préoccupation majeure du gouvernement nigérien. À cet effet, plusieurs conventions ont été ratifiées, notamment l’intégration dans le droit national de la convention africaine sur le déplacement forcé à travers la loi 2018-74 portant protection et assistance aux personnes déplacées internes (PDI) au Niger. Cette loi, en son article 12, rend obligatoire l’enregistrement et l’évaluation des besoins des PDI et promeut également la mise en œuvre de solutions durables. Après plusieurs années de déplacements prolongés, il apparaît nécessaire de réorienter les interventions vers les trois options de solutions durables au déplacement, à savoir : le retour volontaire dans la localité d’origine, l’intégration locale sur le lieu de déplacement et la réinstallation dans une autre localité. Dans cette perspective, le ministère de la Population, de l’Action Sociale et de la Solidarité Nationale, avec l’appui technique et financier de l’OIM, a mené une enquête sur les intentions futures des PDI dans la région de Maradi. Cette enquête vise à identifier les préférences des populations déplacées quant aux options de solutions durables, ainsi qu’à analyser les facteurs motivant leur choix et les implications potentielles qui en découlent. Les données collectées permettent de mieux orienter les stratégies de planification et de programmation en faveur de solutions adaptées au contexte et aux aspirations des communautés concernées. Les principaux résultats indiquent une forte tendance en faveur de l’intégration locale, qui représente 98 pour cent de l’échantillon total, contre 2 pour cent exprimant l’intention de retourner dans leur localité d’origine. L’analyse chronologique des déplacements montre que parmi les personnes enquêtées, 72 pour cent se sont déplacées entre 2023 et 2025, principalement à la suite des attaques de groupes armés non-étatiques (GANE) dans les communes frontalières avec le Nigéria. Au total, 2 pour cent des ménages ont été déplacé avant 2018, 2 pour cent en 2018, 1 pour cent en 2019, 5 pour cent en 2020, 5 pour cent en 2021, 13 pour cent en 2022, 20 pour cent en 2023, puis 33 pour cent en 2024 et 19 pour cent en 2025. Le choix majoritaire de l’intégration locale s’explique principalement par la persistance du sentiment de sécurité dans les localités de déplacement et par le manque d’opportunités socio-économiques dans les localités d’origine. Cependant, le choix de l’intégration locale pose également plusieurs défis, notamment en matière d’accès aux moyens de subsistance, aux opportunités économiques et à la cohésion sociale avec les communautés hôtes.

items.title: Over four children killed or injured on average a day during the ‘so- called’ ceasefire in Lebanon
items.description:
Country: Lebanon
Source: Save the Children

BEIRUT, 12 May 2026 – More than four children have been killed or injured every day on average in Lebanon in the first 25 days of a temporary ceasefire with families still unable to return to their homes, said Save the Children ahead of further peace talks this week. [1]

New data from Lebanon’s Ministry of Health on Tuesday showed that 22 children have been killed and 89 injured since the temporary ceasefire started on 17 April. This brings the number of children killed in Israeli strikes since renewed escalation in hostilities in Lebanon on 2 March to almost 200 with about 2,900 people killed.

The violence and renewed displacement orders have forced more than one million people – or one in six of the population – from their homes with many now living with relatives, in host communities or in collective shelters.

The number of families living in collective shelters has increased 5% since the conditional ceasefire due to renewed displacement orders by Israeli forces and as families return home to find destroyed houses and damaged farmland so move back the collective shelters. There are now 44,800 children among about 125,000 people in collective shelters. [3]

Thousands of children have been living in collective shelters for over two months in overcrowded conditions with inadequate water, sanitation and hygiene facilities leading to reports of scabies and growing health concerns.

Parents are reporting widespread behavioural changes among children living in collective shelters due to a lack of routine and reduced school engagement including loss of appetite and trouble sleeping. Many children are struggling to continue learning with some schools used as collective shelters and also difficulties accessing online learning due to limited electricity, and poor connectivity.

Tala*, 10, has been living in a collective shelter after being displaced from southern Lebanon, said:

“I just want the war to end so I can go home to my village and sleep in my own bed. I really miss school, I want to see my teachers and be with my friends, and study and play again.”

Nora Ingdal, Save the Children’s Lebanon Country Director, said:

“This ‘so called’ ceasefire that still sees more than four children killed or injured every day is not a ceasefire for children. Attacks on civilians have not stopped - it has simply continued under another name. Colleagues have told me that the airstrikes feel more intense in some areas than they ever did before. Children are not safe until there is a permanent and definitive ceasefire with no violations.”

With further peace talks set to take place on Thursday to determine next steps between Lebanon and Israel, Save the Children is calling on the international community to urgently work toward a permanent and definitive ceasefire and ensure flexible and sustained funding to protect children and allow families to return home to resume their lives.

Save the Children has worked in Lebanon since 1953. In collaboration with partners and local authorities, we are distributing essential items in hard-to-reach areas in the south, provide psychosocial support for children, educate families and children about the risks of unexploded ordnance, ensure access to safe water and sanitation facilities, and distribute essential items for those displaced.

ENDS:

Sources:

[1] Lebanon Ministry of Health

Israeli strikes have killed 380 in Lebanon since truce: Health ministry

[2] Lebanon Ministry of Health

[3] Lebanon-Emergency-Sitrep-23-2026.pdf

Notes to editors:

Calculations from Ministry of Health data based on 89 children injured and 22 children killed over 25 days between 17 April until 12 May. In total 199 children have been killed with 177 children before the ceasefire (from 2 March).

items.title: World: Help Logistics Annual Report 2025
items.description:
Country: World
Source: HELP Logistics

Please refer to the attached file.

Mission

We are a catalyst for transferring supply chain and logistics knowledge, strengthening local capacity, and co-creating sustainable solutions to enhance livelihoods and economic resilience in vulnerable communities.

Approach

We drive systemic and lasting impact by solving complex supply chain challenges through independent expertise, data-driven solutions, and collaborative partnerships across humanitarian, development, commercial, and government sectors.

Working alongside partners, we help organizations respond effectively to complex logistics needs while reinforcing commitments to locally driven and sustainable development — co-creating scalable solutions that strengthen supply chains for humanitarian and economic progress.

Ultimately, we help build a sustainable and equitable supply chain network that strengthens local humanitarian response and enhances economic resilience

items.title: World: L'IFRC renforce la préparation aux catastrophes en Asie-Pacifique, optimisant l’impact des investissements humanitaires. | IFRC
items.description:
Country: World
Source: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Kuala Lumpur / Genève, 7 mai 2026 — Alors que les besoins humanitaires augmentent plus rapidement que les ressources disponibles, l’IFRC renforce la préparation aux catastrophes en Asie-Pacifique afin de garantir une action plus précoce, une réponse plus rapide et une meilleure utilisation des financements humanitaires.

Entre juillet 2025 et avril 2026, le programme « Devenir prêt face aux catastrophes » de préparation et de renforcement de la capacité de réponse des Sociétés nationales en Asie-Pacifique a accéléré la transition d’une réponse réactive aux crises vers un système de préparation coordonné à l’échelle régionale et axé sur l’anticipation. Le programme s’aligne sur le Plan stratégique 2025–2028 de préparation des Sociétés nationales en Asie-Pacifique et soutient les Sociétés nationales afin qu’elles puissent anticiper les risques, mobiliser plus rapidement les ressources et répondre à grande échelle lorsque des catastrophes surviennent.

« Chaque franc suisse investi dans la préparation est un choix visant à sauver davantage de vies avec moins de ressources », a déclaré Alexander Matheou, directeur régional de l’IFRC pour l’Asie-Pacifique. « En renforçant les systèmes, la coordination et la préparation avant qu’une catastrophe ne survienne, les Sociétés nationales peuvent répondre plus rapidement, éviter des retards coûteux et accroître efficacement leurs opérations lorsque les besoins sont les plus importants. À une époque marquée par l’augmentation des risques et la réduction des financements humanitaires, la préparation est l’un des investissements les plus responsables et les plus efficaces que nous puissions faire. »

Des systèmes plus solides, des coûts de réponse réduits.

L’un des principaux accomplissements du programme a été la mise en place d’une structure régionale de gestion et de coordination de la préparation aux catastrophes, réunissant l’IFRC et les Sociétés nationales partenaires au sein d’un Groupe de travail central de préparation des Sociétés nationales Asie-Pacifique. Cette structure améliore l’alignement du soutien technique, réduit les duplications et pose les bases d’un futur Hub régional de préparation en Asie-Pacifique afin de faciliter les échanges entre pairs et l’adaptation des outils de préparation selon les contextes.

En 2025, l’IFRC a également achevé une cartographie régionale des capacités et ressources de préparation et de réponse, notamment les Centres d’opérations d’urgence, les Équipes nationales de réponse aux catastrophes, les capacités de déploiement rapide et les Unités d’intervention d’urgence. Associée à une cartographie du soutien des donateurs, cette analyse fournit une base factuelle claire pour orienter les investissements là où ils offrent le meilleur rendement en matière de préparation, afin de garantir une utilisation stratégique et efficace des financements.

Une préparation qui permet des réponses plus rapides.

La préparation opérationnelle a été renforcée grâce à l’outil « Surge Ready », développé en Asie-Pacifique et désormais adopté à l’échelle mondiale. Cet outil fournit un cadre commun pour évaluer et améliorer la préparation aux urgences des Sociétés nationales, des Unités d’intervention d’urgence et des délégations de l’IFRC, conformément aux normes minimales de qualité et de protection approuvées en 2025. Une meilleure préparation des capacités de déploiement permet des interventions plus rapides, une meilleure coordination et une réduction des retards en situation d’urgence.

La région a également renforcé la préparation des Unités d’intervention d’urgence, plusieurs Sociétés nationales ayant amélioré leurs processus de certification et leur coopération entre pairs, contribuant ainsi aux capacités de déploiement régionales et mondiales. En Asie du Sud-Est, les discussions autour de la création d’une Unité régionale WASH d’intervention d’urgence permettraient de renforcer davantage la préparation collective et de réduire la dépendance aux dispositifs ad hoc lors de crises majeures.

Une préparation qui va au-delà des capacités de réponse d’urgence.

Les investissements réalisés dans le cadre du programme vont au-delà des équipements et des équipes d’intervention. Les Sociétés nationales ont renforcé leur préparation dans plusieurs domaines clés : santé et préparation aux pandémies, eau, assainissement et hygiène (WASH), action anticipative, préparation aux transferts monétaires, droit des catastrophes et rôle d’auxiliaire, logistique, protection, genre et inclusion, ainsi qu’engagement communautaire et redevabilité, autant d’éléments essentiels pour garantir une réponse efficace et responsable.

Les données sur les capacités montrent que, même si la plupart des Sociétés nationales sont opérationnellement prêtes à intervenir, le niveau de préparation reste inégal dans des domaines tels que la planification de contingence, l’action précoce et les systèmes facilitants. Combler ces lacunes avant qu’une catastrophe ne survienne permet de réduire les risques opérationnels et d’éviter des coûts de réponse plus élevés par la suite.

Suivre l’impact et orienter les investissements futurs.

L’impact du programme est mesuré à travers une cartographie annuelle des capacités et le suivi des progrès du cycle PER, consolidés dans un tableau de bord régional permettant de suivre l’évolution des niveaux de préparation d’une année à l’autre ainsi que les lacunes persistantes. Cela permet à l’IFRC et à ses partenaires de démontrer les résultats obtenus, de prioriser les investissements et de maximiser les effets des efforts de préparation.

En renforçant la préparation avant que les crises ne surviennent, les investissements de l’IFRC en matière de préparation en Asie-Pacifique permettent d’obtenir un impact humanitaire plus important pour chaque franc investi, aidant ainsi les Sociétés nationales à répondre plus rapidement, de manière mieux coordonnée et à grande échelle dans un environnement de risques de plus en plus complexe.

Pour plus d'informations ou pour demander une interview, merci de nous écrire à l'adresse: [email protected]

A Kuala Lumpur: Afrhill Rances, +60 19 271 3641

A Genève: Scott Craig, +41 76 370 3575

items.title: World: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 12 May 2026
items.description:
Countries: World, Australia, Cook Islands, French Polynesia (France), Guam, Kiribati, Micronesia (Federated States of), New Caledonia (France), New Zealand, Palau, Tonga, United States of America, Vanuatu
Source: Pacific Community

Please refer to the attached Map.

Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 05 May 2026:

Dengue

  • New Caledonia: As of 11 May 2026, dengue transmission remains ongoing in New Caledonia. A total of 1,508 cases have been reported since January 2026, showing a continued increase since the previous update. Transmission is driven by locally acquired cases. DENV‐1 remains the predominant circulating serotype. Seasonal conditions, including rainfall and standing water, continue to favour mosquito breeding. Population movement between communes may also contribute to ongoing transmission. Health authorities continue to encourage early consultation for compatible symptoms and to reinforce vector control measures. The red alert for DENV-1 remains in effect. – Source: La dengue | Direction des Affaires Sanitaires et Sociales de Nouvelle-Calédonie accessed on 12 May 2026.
  • Samoa: As of EpiWeek 18 (27 April–3 May 2026), dengue transmission continues in Samoa, with 153 new cases reported during the week, including 20 laboratory-confirmed cases, representing a decrease compared with the previous week. Six new hospital admissions were recorded, with no ICU admissions and no deaths. DENV-1 (54%) and DENV-2 (46%) continue to co-circulate, and transmission remains widespread across Upolu (64%) and Savai‘i (36%). Children under 15 years (74%) remain the most affected. Since 1st January 2025, a total of 18,712 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,406 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported. The cumulative number of dengue-related deaths remains nine. The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 remains in effect. – Source: Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report in Samoa Issue No 55 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 08 May 2026*.*
  • Tonga: As of 12 May 2026, dengue transmission continues in Tonga following the outbreak declared in April, with a total of 60 laboratory‐confirmed cases, including two new cases since the 08 May. No cases are currently hospitalised, and no dengue‐related deaths have been reported. Children aged 11–14 years remain the most affected, with a predominance of male cases, and most infections continue to be reported from Tongatapu. DENV‐2 is the identified circulating serotype. The red alert for DENV-2 remains in effect. – Source: Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report #19 - Ministry of Health Tonga - Official Facebook page accessed on 12 May 2026.

Pertussis/Whooping cough

  • Guam: As of 7 May 2026 (EpiWeek 18), pertussis transmission continues in Guam, with a total of 24 cases detected since EpiWeek 15 of 2025, including 23 laboratory-confirmed cases and one probable case. Two new laboratory-confirmed cases were identified on 1 and 2 May 2026. One case has been hospitalised, with no pertussis-related deaths reported. A total of 15 cases have been reported in 2026 to date. Cases affect a wide age range, including infants, children, and adults, with several cases affecting unvaccinated or partially vaccinated persons. Public health authorities continue case investigation, contact tracing, post-exposure prophylaxis, and vaccination activities, with a focus on infants, pregnant women, and close contacts, while efforts to improve DTaP vaccination coverage remain ongoing. The red alert for pertussis remains in effec*t. – Sources: Pertussis Outbreak Situation Report N°6 shared on PacNet on 08 May 2026 and* DPHSS Update_Guam Pertussis_05.11.26 and Epi Reports | Epidemiology and Laboratory Capacity (ELC) Guam – DPHSS accessed on 05 May 2026.
  • New Zealand: Pertussis activity in New Zealand continues as part of the ongoing national epidemic, with transmission persisting nationwide. As of the week ending 1 May 2026, 31 cases were reported, a decrease compared with 39 cases in the previous week. This brings the cumulative total for 2026 to 642 cases. Weekly notifications remain lower than earlier in the year but indicate sustained transmission. Since the start of the epidemic in October 2024, a total of 4,400 confirmed, probable, and suspected cases have been notified, with 9.6% requiring hospitalisation and one death reported. Infants under one year of age remain the most vulnerable group, reinforcing the importance of vaccination, surveillance, and protection of high‐risk populations. The blue alert for pertussis remains in effect. – Sources: Pertussis dashboard and Pertussis Report 4 April–1 May 2026 accessed on 12 May 2026.
  • Vanuatu: As of EpiWeeks 17–18, 2026, pertussis transmission in Vanuatu remains ongoing but continues to show a declining trend compared with the peak observed in late 2025. One new clinically diagnosed case was reported during this period, from Efate, and the last laboratory-confirmed cases were reported in EpiWeek 14. The cumulative total has increased to 829 cases, comprising 785 clinically diagnosed and 44 laboratory-confirmed cases. The cumulative number of deaths remains seven, including six deaths in Tanna and one in Shefa, with no current hospitalisations reported. Cases affect all age groups, with the highest burden among children aged 1–9 years, and a substantial proportion among infants under one year, while relatively few cases are reported among individuals aged 10 years and above. The sex distribution remains approximately equal. Among cases with known vaccination status, 48% were unvaccinated, while 35% had received one dose, 6% two doses, and 11% three doses of a pertussis-containing vaccine. Transmission remains concentrated in Efate (~54%) and Tanna (~38%). Although case numbers continue to decline, they remain above the outbreak threshold, and enhanced surveillance and catch-up immunisation activities continue. The blue alert for pertussis remains in effect. – Source: Source: Vanuatu Pertussis Outbreak: Situation Report 19 (04 May 2026) shared on PacNet on 08 May 2026.

Measles

  • Australia: Measles activity continues in Australia, with most cases linked to imported infections and secondary transmission. As of early May 2026, cases have been reported primarily in New South Wales and Victoria, with recent cases associated with international travel to South‐East Asia. In New South Wales, multiple cases in Sydney and the Sutherland Shire were infectious while visiting healthcare facilities and public venues, prompting exposure alerts and public health follow‐up. A total of 47 measles cases have been confirmed in NSW since 1 January 2026, indicating an increased risk of transmission. National surveillance data show that cases continue to occur across several jurisdictions, predominantly among under‐vaccinated individuals or those with unknown vaccination status. The red alert for measles remains in effect. – Sources: Measles alert for Sydney, Measles alert for Sutherland Shire and National Communicable Diseases Surveillance Dashboard accessed on 12 May 2026.

Rotavirus

  • Kiribati: As of 05 May 2026, the rotavirus outbreak in Kiribati remains ongoing but shows a continued declining trend. A total of 6,342 cumulative diarrhoea cases have been reported nationwide, indicating limited additional case accrual since the previous update. Surveillance data confirm that the outbreak peaked in early March, with case numbers steadily decreasing from mid‐ to late March, although low‐level transmission persists. Transmission continues to be concentrated in South Tarawa, particularly Betio, which accounts for the highest burden of cases. Children under five years of age remain the most affected group. The cumulative death toll remains unchanged, with three confirmed deaths and two suspected deaths under investigation. Enhanced surveillance, laboratory testing, health promotion activities, and community‐level hygiene interventions, particularly focusing on child health, remain in place. The blue alert for rotavirus remains in effect. – Source: Rotavirus Outbreak Situational Report 20 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 12 May 2026.

Other Information:

Dengue

  • New Zealand: In New Zealand (NZ), during EpiWeek 18 (02 – 08 May 2026), two confirmed imported dengue cases were reported. The two cases had returned from Samoa. Given the known serological cross‐reactivity between dengue and other flaviviruses, some reported dengue cases may ultimately be reclassified as other flavivirus infections as epidemiological and laboratory investigations continue. – Sources: NZ Arbovirus Notifications by Country (18: 02/05/2026–08/05/2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 11 May 2026.

Diarrhoea

  • Federated States of Micronesia: Diarrhoeal activity continues in Chuuk State in the aftermaths of typhoon Sinlaku, with cases reported through routine syndromic surveillance. While overall diarrhoeal activity has fluctuated in recent weeks, transmission remains a concern due to ongoing environmental factors and challenges related to water safety. One cholera case was laboratory confirmed on 23 April 2026, prompting immediate public health investigation, including assessment of potential water sources. The strain identified is non-epidemic, and therefore no outbreak has been declared. Health authorities continue enhanced surveillance, case investigation, and risk communication, alongside efforts to strengthen water, sanitation, and hygiene (WASH) measures, particularly in communities affected by Typhoon Sinlaku. – Sources: CDEOC Sinlaku - SitRep 8.pdf accessed on 12 May 2026 & Yap State Department of Health Services, Communicable Disease report (Week #18; 27 April–03 Mayl 2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body focal point on 06 May 2026.

Hantavirus – Andes virus

  • Cruise ship (International): Hantavirus activity has been reported in multiple countries following a cluster linked to cruise ship travel on the MV Hondius, involving the Andes virus, one of the very few Hantaviruses associated with documented human-to-human transmission. As of 8 May 2026, eight cases, including three deaths, have been reported among passengers and crew, with secondary international travel occurring after disembarkation. In Singapore, two residents with exposure to the vessel were isolated and tested, with both testing negative for hantavirus and the overall public health risk assessed as low. In French Polynesia, a case contact transited through Tahiti and Mangareva before authorities were notified; the individual remains asymptomatic and isolated in quarantine in Pitcairn Island, with precautionary measures implemented to prevent onward travel. Australia has announced the repatriation of several nationals and one New Zealander previously onboard the vessel, none of whom are symptomatic, with all individuals transferred under protective measures to quarantine facilities in Western Australia for monitoring. WHO continues to assess the global public health risk as low, while emphasising the importance of early case detection, isolation of suspect cases and international contact tracing given the long incubation period but limited human-to-human transmission potential of Andes virus. – Sources: Hantavirus cluster linked to cruise ship travel, Multi-country, Both Singapore residents onboard MV Hondius tested negative for hantavirus | Communicable Diseases Agency, Un cas contact d'Hantavirus a transité par Tahiti puis Mangareva le 7 mai and WA Health update regarding Hantavirus situation accessed on 12 May 2026.

Measles

  • Bangladesh: Bangladesh continues to experience a severe nationwide measles surge, with widespread transmission reported across all divisions. Since mid‐March 2026, a cumulative total of over 41,000 suspected measles cases has been reported, with more than 5,000 laboratory‐confirmed cases and hundreds of suspected measles‐related deaths, placing significant pressure on health services. Daily case counts remain high, exceeding 1,300 cases per day, indicating sustained community transmission, particularly in Dhaka, Rajshahi and Chattogram divisions. In response, the Government, with support from UNICEF, WHO, Gavi and partners, launched an emergency measles–rubella vaccination campaign on 5 April 2026, targeting children aged 6 months to 5 years in high‐risk districts, with nationwide scale‐up initiated from May. However, vaccination coverage remains below target, with immunity gaps persisting due to missed routine immunisation, especially in densely populated urban areas and humanitarian settings. Authorities and partners continue to prioritise mass vaccination, enhanced surveillance, and community engagement, warning that without rapid improvement in coverage, transmission and severe outcomes among children are likely to continue. – Source: IFRC GO - Field Report Details accessed on 12 May 2026.
  • Japan: Measles activity continues to increase in Japan in early 2026, despite the country having maintained measles elimination status since 2015. As of EpiWeek 17 (ending 26 April 2026), a cumulative total of 436 cases has been reported, showing a sustained upward trend since February. Cases have been notified from multiple prefectures, including Tokyo, Kanagawa, Aichi, Osaka and Saitama, with both imported infections and domestic transmission identified. Surveillance data indicate that a substantial proportion of cases occurred among unvaccinated or partially vaccinated individuals, while infections have been reported across a wide age range, including young adults and school‐aged children. Health authorities continue to strengthen risk communication, urging the public and healthcare providers to verify measles–rubella vaccination status, ensure two‐dose coverage, and seek medical care promptly while avoiding public transport if symptoms develop, particularly in the context of ongoing international measles activity and increased population movement. – Source: meas26-17 accessed on 12 Mayl 2026.
  • New Zealand: In April 2026, New Zealand reported a locally acquired measles case in Wellington with no identified links to overseas travel or previously known cases. The individual was infectious while visiting several public settings, prompting the identification of multiple locations of interest across the lower North Island and initiation of contact tracing, public exposure notifications and enhanced surveillance. As of 11 May 2026, one additional case has been confirmed, bringing the total number of reported measles cases in the greater Wellington region to four. Health New Zealand has indicated that measles is likely circulating in the local community, and investigations are ongoing to establish epidemiological links between cases. Public health authorities continue to urge the public to verify their measles–mumps–rubella (MMR) vaccination status, monitor for symptoms, and follow guidance related to locations of interest, while free MMR vaccination has been extended to people aged 12 months and over in affected areas. – Sources: Third measles case confirmed in Wellington & Fourth case of measles in Wellington accessed on 12 May 2026.
